text:
For an initial exegetical exploration of Romans 8.3–4 see in particular Gaffin who writes, “The solution to the problems identified . . . as presented by Paul corresponds to each element of the problems as identified by Paul.” Paul’s solution to the problems of sin with its attendant consequence identifies the three elements of the problems identified by Paul. The phrase τὸ . . . ἀδύνατον is an accusative of respect (as argued in contrast to an anacoluthon) and so serves to remind Paul’s readers of the three aspects of humanity’s plight as so carefully identified in Romans 7.7–25: (1) the controlling power of sinful temptations, (2) the carnal nature of human flesh, and (3) the fact that human beings are unable to do what is righteous under the law.

The study concludes that it is God who has brought the solution to the impasse: it is through the mission of his Son that God acts in Christ through the likeness of sinful flesh by destroying the power of sin. Consequently, it is through Christ’s fulfilling of the law for those now living in the Spirit that God also brings to an end the powerlessness of the law. The conclusion is reached that Romans 8.3–4 forms a crucial transitional phase in the author’s exploration of humanity’s situation in the old world and its entrance into the new world of participation in the divine life in Christ.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
